fractured.title: Wrist Fracture Recovery Plan
steps: Immobilization: Wear the provided splint or cast at all times as directed.
Elevation: Keep your wrist elevated above heart level for the first 3 days.
Ice Therapy: Apply cold packs for 15 mins every few hours to reduce swelling.
Finger Motion: Regularly wiggle fingers to maintain circulation and flexibility.
Follow-up: Schedule orthopedic review within 7-10 days for X-ray monitoring.
Warning: Seek help for blue fingers, extreme numbness, or severe throbbing.

fractured.title: Hand Fracture Recovery Guide
steps: Splinting: Use the prescribed hand or finger splint to maintain bone alignment.
Elevation: Rest the hand on pillows above your heart whenever possible.
Swelling Control: Apply ice in a protector for 10-15 mins several times daily.
Activity Limit: Avoid all lifting or gripping tasks with the affected hand.
Therapy: Begin gentle finger gliding exercises only when specifically cleared.
Alert: Contact your doctor if you lose the ability to move your fingers.

fractured.title: Finger Fracture Care Plan
steps: Buddy Taping/Splinting: Keep the finger secured to its neighbor or in its splint.
Ice: Use small cold packs on the injured joint for 10 mins multiple times a day.
Elevation: Keep the hand raised; do not let it hang by your side for long periods.
Protection: Avoid high-impact activities or sports that could restrike the finger.
Flexibility: Once stable, gentle range-of-motion starts as advised by your PT.
Signs: Report any severe deformity or skin breakdown around the splint.

fractured.title: Forearm Fracture Protocol
steps: Sling Support: Consistently use your sling to support the weight of the cast.
Weight Limit: Strictly no lifting or pushing with the injured arm.
Cast Maintenance: Keep the cast dry and clean; never insert objects inside.
Circulation: Check for warmth and color in your hand and fingers regularly.
X-ray Schedule: Monitoring usually occurs at weeks 2, 6, and 12.
Emergency: Sudden chest pain or shortness of breath requires immediate ER visit.

fractured.title: Elbow Fracture Recovery
steps: Immobilization: Use the long-arm splint or sling to keep the joint at 90 degrees.
Pain Management: Use ice and prescribed medication to manage acute pain.
Stiffness Prevention: Gently move your wrist and fingers to prevent distal stiffness.
No Weight Bearing: Do not use the arm to push up from a chair or bed.
Physical Therapy: Crucial for elbow recovery; begin exercises exactly as scheduled.
Nerve Watch: Report any tingling or 'funny bone' sensation in your pinky finger.

fractured.title: Upper Arm (Humerus) Recovery
steps: Hanging Sling: Gravity often helps alignment; wear your brace or sling correctly.
Sleeping: Sleep in a reclined or upright position to minimize arm movement.
No Leaning: Do not rest on your elbow or lean the weight of the arm on surfaces.
Pendulum Work: When cleared, perform small circular swings to prevent 'frozen shoulder'.
Activity: Avoid driving until your doctor confirms significant callus formation.
Watch: Report any new weakness in extending your wrist or fingers.

fractured.title: Shoulder Fracture Management
steps: Shoulder Immobilizer: Keep the arm strapped to your torso as instructed.
Comfort: Propping the elbow with a pillow while seated can reduce shoulder strain.
Heat/Cold: Ice for the first 72 hours, then warm compresses for muscle spasms.
Posture: Maintain an upright posture to prevent secondary neck and back pain.
Rehab: Follow a strict PT protocol to regain range of motion safely.
Warning: Sudden swelling in the arm or neck requires medical evaluation.

fractured.title: Hip Fracture Recovery
steps: Early Mobilization: Stand and walk with assistance within 24 hours of surgery.
Hip Precautions: Avoid bending the hip past 90 degrees and do not cross legs.
Anticoagulation: Take blood-thinning medication as prescribed to prevent clots.
Physical Therapy: Daily exercises are mandatory to regain leg strength.
Home Safety: Use high-seated chairs and a raised toilet seat.
Complications: Watch for calf pain, shortness of breath, or surgical site redness.

fractured.title: Leg (Femur) Recovery
steps: Non-Weight Bearing: Strictly no pressure on the leg; use crutches or a walker.
Elevation: Keep the leg propped on pillows above heart level while resting.
Thrombo-care: Perform ankle pumps (if allowed) to maintain lower leg circulation.
Skin Care: Monitor for pressure sores under the cast or brace edges.
Nutrition: Ensure adequate intake of protein, calcium, and Vitamin D.
Alert: Seek help for severe swelling, calf heat, or chest pain.

fractured.title: Knee (Patella) Recovery
steps: Bracing: Keep the knee brace locked in extension if that was prescribed.
Straight Leg Raises: Perform isometric quad sets to prevent muscle wasting.
Icing: Apply ice to the knee for 20 mins every 4 hours for the first week.
Weight Status: Strictly adhere to the weight-bearing limits (often Toe-Touch).
Stairs: Use the 'Up with the Good, Down with the Bad' technique for stairs.
Urgency: Report any locking, clicking, or sudden inability to move the ankle.

fractured.title: Tibia (Shin Bone) Protocol
steps: Casting: Maintain the long-leg or short-leg cast; keep it dry at all times.
Elevation: Crucial in the first 2 weeks to prevent throbbing pain and swelling.
Weight Transition: Move to partial weight-bearing only when callus is seen on X-ray.
Toes: Wiggle toes constantly to check for nerve function and blood flow.
Safety: Remove area rugs and obstacles from walking paths in your home.
Complication: Report 'Compartment Syndrome' signs: intense pain even with meds.

fractured.title: Ankle Fracture Recovery
steps: RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, and Elevation are vital for the first 14 days.
Weight Bearing: Do not attempt to walk on the ankle until cleared by a doctor.
Orthopedic Boot: If provided a boot, wear it consistently for all movements.
Gentle Mobilization: Start ankle circles only after the fracture has stabilized.
PT Focus: Work on balance and proprioception to prevent future sprains.
Nerve Watch: Watch for numbness on the top or bottom of the foot.

fractured.title: Foot Fracture Recovery Guide
steps: Protective Footwear: Use a stiff-soled shoe or walking boot as directed.
Elevation: Keeping the foot raised is the best way to manage foot pain.
Non-Weight Bearing: Use a knee scooter or crutches if total rest is required.
Swelling Monitor: Foot swelling can last for months; continue elevation as needed.
Arch Support: Transition to supportive sneakers once the boot is removed.
Alert: Report any skin changes, ulcers, or severe burning pain.

fractured.title: Toe Fracture Care Plan
steps: Buddy Taping: Secure the injured toe to the healthy one next to it with padding.
Stiff Shoes: Wear shoes with a hard sole that prevents the toe from bending.
Ice: Apply small ice packs for 10-15 mins several times daily.
Rest: Minimize walking and avoid sports for at least 3-4 weeks.
Elevation: Propping your foot up while sitting will reduce throbbing.
Signs: See a doctor if the toe is severely crooked or if there is an open wound.

fractured.title: Spine (Vertebral) Recovery
steps: Bracing: Wear your back brace (TLSO/LSO) exactly as instructed, especially when up.
BLT Rules: No Bending, No Lifting (over 5 lbs), and No Twisting the spine.
Log Rolling: Use the log-rolling technique to get in and out of bed.
Walking: Short, frequent walks on flat surfaces are the best early exercise.
Pain Control: Management may include nerve blocks or specific medications.
Urgent: Seek ER care for bowel/bladder changes or sudden leg weakness.
